findings.description: Direct contradiction: Overview states 'All pipeline stages run in parallel to maximize throughput' but Stage 2 states 'Stage 2 must complete before Stage 3 begins.' These two claims are mutually exclusive.
findings.evidence: Overview: 'All pipeline stages run in parallel to maximize throughput.' Stage 2: 'Stage 2 must complete before Stage 3 begins.'
findings.suggestion: Remove or replace the 'all stages run in parallel' claim in the Overview with an accurate description of the pipeline's execution model, acknowledging the Stage 2→3 sequential dependency.

findings.description: 'Security considerations are documented in Section 6' — but no Section 6 exists anywhere in the document. The document ends with Conclusions as the last section.
findings.evidence: Stage 4: 'Security considerations are documented in Section 6.' Document sections present: Overview, Architecture Components (Stages 1–4), Performance Characteristics, Operational Notes, Conclusions. No Section 6 exists.
findings.suggestion: Either remove the reference and add a Security section, or remove the reference entirely and address authentication/security inline.

findings.description: No security section exists in the document. The reference to 'Section 6' for security considerations is a dangling reference pointing to absent content. For a final architecture brief, security must be documented.
findings.evidence: Stage 4 references 'Section 6' for security. No such section exists. The only security-relevant content in the document is the single vague line 'Authentication is used to verify each request before processing begins' in Stage 1.
findings.suggestion: Add a Security section covering at minimum: authentication mechanism, authorization model, data in transit encryption, and any relevant compliance considerations.

findings.description: 'Benchmarks show that batch processing is 3× faster than streaming for files under 10MB.' This claim appears factually backwards. For small, frequent payloads (under 10MB), streaming typically outperforms batch because batch processing introduces accumulation latency and overhead for small payloads. Batch processing advantages (throughput amortization) manifest at larger scales.
findings.evidence: Performance Characteristics: 'Benchmarks show that batch processing is 3× faster than streaming for files under 10MB. For larger payloads, streaming is preferred.' The second sentence also contradicts standard ETL practice where batch excels at large-volume throughput, not small-file speed.
findings.suggestion: Correct the claim: streaming typically offers lower latency for small, frequent events; batch processing offers higher throughput for large payloads. If the benchmark figure is real and specific to this system, provide the source or methodology.

findings.description: 'The in-memory delivery cache reduces end-to-end latency by 99.9% compared to unbuffered writes.' A 99.9% reduction means latency is cut by a factor of 1000×. This is an extraordinary, unsupported claim with no benchmark methodology, baseline figure, or source provided.
findings.evidence: Stage 3: 'The in-memory delivery cache reduces end-to-end latency by 99.9% compared to unbuffered writes.'
findings.suggestion: Remove the specific percentage or replace with a sourced, defensible figure. If the intent is to indicate significant improvement, use qualified language ('significantly reduces latency') or provide a realistic benchmark with methodology.

findings.description: 'Under nominal load, the pipeline maintains sub-second end-to-end latency for 95th-percentile events' but Stage 3 states 'Polling for batch completion uses a 5-minute interval.' If events are written in micro-batches and batch completion is polled every 5 minutes, end-to-end latency for confirmed delivery cannot be sub-second for most events.
findings.evidence: Performance Characteristics: 'sub-second end-to-end latency for 95th-percentile events.' Stage 3: 'Polling for batch completion uses a 5-minute interval.'
findings.suggestion: Clarify what 'end-to-end latency' measures: time-to-queue-write vs. time-to-confirmed-warehouse-delivery. If the sub-second claim applies only to ingestion-to-transformation, scope it explicitly. If it covers full pipeline latency, reconcile with the 5-minute polling interval.

findings.description: 'Transformation applies the standard retry policy on transient failures.' The 'standard retry policy' is never defined anywhere in the document — no retry interval, backoff strategy, or attempt count is specified for this policy.
findings.evidence: Stage 2: 'Transformation applies the standard retry policy on transient failures.' No definition of 'standard retry policy' appears elsewhere in the document. (Note: Stage 3 specifies '3 retry attempts' for delivery but this is a different, explicitly scoped retry, not the 'standard' policy.)
findings.suggestion: Define the standard retry policy inline or in an Operational Notes section: specify retry count, backoff strategy (e.g., exponential backoff), and initial delay interval.

findings.description: 'Large files are handled separately from the primary transformation path via an overflow queue.' 'Large' is never defined — no file size threshold is specified to distinguish normal from large files.
findings.evidence: Stage 2: 'Large files are handled separately from the primary transformation path via an overflow queue.' No size threshold defined anywhere in the document.
findings.suggestion: Define the size threshold that triggers the overflow queue (e.g., 'Files exceeding 100MB are routed to the overflow queue').

findings.description: 'pipeline' and 'workflow' are used interchangeably throughout the document with no definition of either term. In Conclusions, 'workflow' and 'pipeline' appear to refer to distinct concepts ('a real-time streaming workflow alongside the existing batch pipeline'), but earlier uses treat them as synonyms ('The pipeline and workflow are monitored by the on-call team').
findings.evidence: Operational Notes: 'The pipeline and workflow are monitored by the on-call team via the Prometheus dashboard.' Conclusions: 'Future work includes adding a real-time streaming workflow alongside the existing batch pipeline.'
findings.suggestion: Define 'pipeline' as the end-to-end batch data processing system described in this document. Use 'workflow' only if referring to a distinct, separately defined process. Revise the Operational Notes bullet to use 'pipeline' consistently.

findings.description: 'ETL' is used without expansion on first use. The target audience includes readers who may not know the acronym.
findings.evidence: Overview: 'an internal ETL system that handles batch ingestion, transformation, and delivery' — first use of ETL with no expansion.
findings.suggestion: Expand on first use: 'Extract, Transform, Load (ETL)'.

findings.description: 'Authentication is used to verify each request before processing begins.' The type of authentication is never specified — API key, JWT, OAuth2, mTLS, and basic auth are all possible and have significantly different security and operational implications.
findings.evidence: Stage 1: 'Authentication is used to verify each request before processing begins.'
findings.suggestion: Specify the authentication mechanism (e.g., 'API key authentication', 'JWT bearer token', 'mutual TLS (mTLS)').
